entries.description: Founding of the first hospitals and hospices: Basil of Caesarea established the Basilias in Caesarea around 370 CE — the first institution in history dedicated to care of the sick regardless of social status, directly inspired by 'I was sick and you visited me' (Matthew 25) and the Good Samaritan parable. This model became the template for all subsequent hospital systems globally. Estimated 500,000 patients served in the first century of operation across early Christian hospital networks in the Byzantine Empire. Confidence MEDIUM: population estimate based on historical records of early Christian philanthropy.
entries.causation_evidence: Historical records of Basil's hospital; Sozomen's Ecclesiastical History; early Church philanthropy documentation; Gary Ferngren 'Medicine and Health Care in Early Christianity' (2009)

entries.description: Abolition of the British slave trade: William Wilberforce and evangelical abolitionists explicitly grounded the campaign in 'love thy neighbor' and the equal dignity of all humans before God. The 1807 Slave Trade Act stopped the transportation of enslaved Africans in British ships. Conservative estimate of 800,000 people directly protected in the immediate post-1807 generation from being transported into enslavement — based on the documented rate of approximately 266,000 Africans transported in the preceding 7 years (1801-1807) projected forward. The 1833 Slavery Abolition Act subsequently freed 800,000 enslaved people already held in British colonies. Confidence MEDIUM: numbers well-documented, causal attribution to Jesus's teachings is high-confidence through Wilberforce's own writings.
entries.causation_evidence: Wilberforce parliamentary records; Royal Museums Greenwich slave trade data; EBSCO Research 1807 Act documentation; Wilberforce's 'Real Christianity' (1797) explicitly citing Gospel imperatives

entries.description: American civil rights movement: Martin Luther King Jr. explicitly and repeatedly grounded nonviolent resistance in the Sermon on the Mount and the commandment to love enemies. The Civil Rights Act of 1964 and Voting Rights Act of 1965 directly resulted from this movement, legally protecting approximately 20 million Black Americans from institutionalized racial discrimination in employment, public accommodation, and voting. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' and 'I Have a Dream' speech are primary documents explicitly citing Jesus's teachings as the moral foundation. Num_affected is the documented Black American population in 1963 whose legal status changed as a direct result.
entries.causation_evidence: MLK 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' April 16 1963; MLK 'Strength to Love' (1963); Civil Rights Act of 1964; US Census Bureau 1960 Black American population data (18.9 million); Library of Congress civil rights documentation

entries.description: Indian independence movement: Mahatma Gandhi drew satyagraha (truth-force) and ahimsa (nonviolence) directly from the Sermon on the Mount. Gandhi wrote explicitly: 'The Sermon on the Mount went straight to my heart.' His nonviolent independence campaign ended British colonial rule over approximately 350 million Indians in 1947. Gandhi is documented as stating Jesus's teachings were among the most direct influences on his philosophy of nonviolent resistance. Num_affected is documented Indian population at independence.
entries.causation_evidence: Gandhi's 'The Story of My Experiments with Truth'; Gandhi's collected writings on Christianity; Census of India 1941 (318 million) projected to 1947 (approx 350 million); Indian Independence Act 1947

entries.description: International Red Cross founding: Henri Dunant was explicitly inspired by Christian charity and the Good Samaritan parable after witnessing the Battle of Solferino in 1859. His 1862 book 'A Memory of Solferino' drew directly on Christian moral imperatives to care for the wounded regardless of nationality. The Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement now operates in 187 countries. Annual beneficiary figure of 229 million used as documented single-year impact (2011 Global Impact Report) — representing ongoing annual surplus generated by this institution. Lifetime cumulative beneficiaries across 160+ years of operation estimated in the billions. Confidence MEDIUM for annual figure; cumulative figure not used to avoid overcounting.
entries.causation_evidence: Dunant 'A Memory of Solferino' (1862); ICRC founding documents 1863; American Red Cross 2011 Global Impact Report (229 million served); Wikipedia ICRC history; Nobel Peace Prize citation 1901

entries.description: Founding of medieval universities: Oxford (1096-1167), Cambridge (1209), University of Paris (1150), Bologna (1088) all established on Christian principles of truth-seeking. These institutions became the foundation of all modern higher education globally. Conservative estimate of 30,000 students enrolled across major medieval universities by 1200 CE. Downstream impact on global knowledge systems immeasurable — entry uses conservative direct enrollment figure only. Confidence MEDIUM.
entries.causation_evidence: Historical charters of Oxford, Cambridge, Paris, Bologna; Charles Homer Haskins 'The Rise of Universities' (1923); UNESCO history of higher education

entries.description: South African Truth and Reconciliation Commission: Archbishop Desmond Tutu explicitly applied Jesus's teachings on forgiveness, reconciliation, and restorative justice as the theological framework for the TRC process. Tutu's concept of ubuntu — 'I am because we are' — was grounded in Christian theology. The TRC addressed the full population of post-apartheid South Africa: approximately 42 million people in 1996 who were affected by or implicated in apartheid. Entry uses total South African population as num_affected, reflecting the civilizational scale of the reconciliation process. Confidence MEDIUM.
entries.causation_evidence: TRC official records; Tutu 'No Future Without Forgiveness' (1999); South African Census 1996; Tutu's explicit theological framing of the TRC process

entries.description: Early Christian systematic philanthropy: The early Church established the first systematic welfare programs in history for orphans, widows, the poor, and the sick — directly commanded by Jesus's teachings (Matthew 25, Luke 4). By 400 CE the Church in Rome alone was supporting approximately 1,500 widows and distressed persons daily. Conservative estimate of 1 million people supported annually across the Roman Empire by Christian charitable networks by 400 CE. This model became the template for all subsequent welfare systems globally. Confidence MEDIUM.
entries.causation_evidence: Early Church fathers' writings; Eusebius 'Ecclesiastical History'; Rodney Stark 'The Rise of Christianity' (1996) documents early Christian welfare networks; historical accounts of Roman Church welfare rolls

entries.description: Universal Declaration of Human Rights: Core principles of inherent human dignity and equality were rooted in Judeo-Christian teachings of imago Dei (humans made in God's image) and Jesus's commandment to love neighbor. Key drafters including Eleanor Roosevelt, René Cassin, and Charles Malik explicitly referenced this tradition. The UDHR was adopted December 10 1948 and established legal protections for every human being alive — world population in 1948 was approximately 2.5 billion. This is the largest single surplus event in the ledger: every human being on earth received a formal legal framework of protection whose moral foundations trace directly to these teachings. Confidence MEDIUM: causal attribution to Jesus's teachings specifically is strong through documented drafting history; population figure is exact.
entries.causation_evidence: UDHR drafting history; Mary Ann Glendon 'A World Made New' (2001) documents Christian influence on UDHR framers; Charles Malik's own writings on Christian foundations of human rights; UN world population data 1948

entries.description: Global missionary hospitals and schools: By 1900 Christian missionaries had established thousands of hospitals, clinics, and schools across Africa, Asia, and Latin America — many in regions with no prior access to formal healthcare or education. Conservative estimate of 10 million patients and students served annually by missionary institutions by 1900. These institutions became the foundation of healthcare and education systems in many developing nations. Confidence MEDIUM: aggregate figure based on missionary society records and WHO historical data.
entries.causation_evidence: International Missionary Council records; WHO historical data on missionary medicine; Dana Robert 'Christian Mission: How Christianity Became a World Religion' (2009); London Missionary Society annual reports
